era_context: The Roman Republic is in the post-Sullan period of recovery and reorganization. Pompey's Hispanian campaign is ongoing (76–71 BCE). The Sertorian War has recently ended (72 BCE). Crassus is consolidating wealth and influence. Caesar is in his mid-fifties, politically active. In China, Emperor Xuan of Han is in his fourth year of rule, consolidating his authority. The Parthian Empire is establishing itself as the dominant Near Eastern power. Egypt is experiencing succession crises and increasing Roman influence.

events.title: Death of Sulla and End of His Dictatorship
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Sulla, the dictator of Rome who had seized power in 82 BCE and implemented sweeping reforms and proscriptions, dies in 78 BCE. Sulla's death marks the formal end of his dictatorship, though he had already retired from active rule in 79 BCE. His death removes a powerful figure from Roman politics and allows for the emergence of new power centers. The post-Sullan period is characterized by attempts to undo some of his reforms and by the rise of new military strongmen like Pompey and Crassus.
key_figures: Lucius Cornelius Sulla (dictator of Rome, r. 82–79 BCE)

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Sulla (written c. 100 CE)
Appian, Roman History, Book I (written c. 160 CE)

events.certainty_note: Sulla's death in 78 BCE is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is certain.

events.title: Lepidus Attempts to Overturn Sullan Reforms
events.description: Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, a consul in 78 BCE, attempts to reverse some of Sulla's reforms, particularly regarding the restoration of power to the tribunes of the plebs. Lepidus's attempt is opposed by the optimates (conservative faction) and fails. However, his actions signal the beginning of a broader movement to undo Sullan reforms. This period marks the beginning of the end of Sullan dominance in Roman politics, though the process will take several more years.

sources.name: Appian, Roman History, Book I (written c. 160 CE)
Plutarch, Life of Pompey (written c. 100 CE)

events.certainty_note: Lepidus's consulship and his attempt to reverse Sullan reforms are well-attested in sources. The date of 78 BCE is confirmed.

events.title: Emperor Xuan of Han Strengthens Confucian Ideology in Governance
events.description: Emperor Xuan continues to promote Confucian ideology as the foundation of Han governance, while maintaining pragmatic legalist administrative practices. He establishes himself as a patron of Confucian scholars and promotes the examination system for selecting officials based on merit and Confucian knowledge. This period marks the strengthening of Confucianism as the state ideology of the Han dynasty, a trend that will continue for centuries.
key_figures: Liu Xun, Emperor Xuan of Han (r. 74–49 BCE)

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, compiled c. 111 CE

events.certainty_note: Emperor Xuan's promotion of Confucianism is well-documented in general terms, but specific actions in 78 BCE are not individually dated.

events.title: Parthian Empire Possibly Experiences Succession Crisis
events.description: If Orodes I's reign ended in 77 BCE, then 78 BCE may mark a succession crisis or the reign of a different Parthian king. The chronology of early Parthian rulers is highly uncertain, and different sources give conflicting dates. This period is characterized by internal consolidation and the establishment of Parthian authority over vassal states. The exact political situation in 78 BCE is unclear due to limited contemporary sources.

sources.name: Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus (compiled c. 200 CE)

events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology for this period is highly uncertain and disputed. The identity and regnal dates of Parthian rulers are not well-established in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Sulla's death in 78 BCE is well-attested, but the significance of his death is sometimes overstated. His reforms had already been implemented and were not immediately reversed; the process of undoing Sullan reforms took several decades. Lepidus's attempt to reverse Sullan reforms is sometimes portrayed as more significant than it actually was; it was a failed attempt that had limited immediate impact. Parthian chronology remains highly disputed; the succession situation in 78 BCE is entirely unclear due to limited sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 78 BCE is moderate but heavily skewed toward Rome and the Mediterranean. Roman sources are later compilations (Plutarch, Appian) rather than contemporary accounts. Chinese sources (Han Shu) are more contemporary but focus on court politics. Parthian history relies on fragmentary Greek and Roman accounts with significant chronological uncertainty. The record is dominated by elite political and military history; economic, social, and technological developments are poorly documented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, Central Asia, and the Americas are entirely absent from surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Sulla's death in 78 BCE enabled Lepidus's attempt to reverse Sullan reforms in 77 BCE
Lepidus's failed attempt to reverse Sullan reforms contributed to the broader movement that eventually succeeded in the 70s BCE
Emperor Xuan's promotion of Confucianism is part of his broader governance
